query: risk of endocarditis in heart conditions such as VSD, MR, AR, ASD

retrieved_docs.title: Embolic Stroke -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: The risk of embolism is variable. The most common potential high-risk cardiac conditions that can cause embolic ischemic stroke include atrial fibrillation (AF), recent myocardial infarction, mechanical prosthetic valve, dilated myocardiopathy, and mitral rheumatic stenosis, respectively. However, other identifiable high-risk cardioembolic sources include infective bacterial endocarditis, nonbacterial marantic endocarditis, and left atrial myxoma, which is a rare condition but the most common tumor originating from the heart. Minor cardioembolism sources include patent foramen ovale (PFO), calcific aortic valve stenosis, mitral valve annular calcification, atrial septal aneurysm, ventricular (VSD), or atrial septal defects (ASD).

retrieved_docs.title: Bacterial Endocarditis -- Epidemiology -- Risk factors
retrieved_docs.content: Age greater than 60 years, male gender, injection drug use, history of prior infective endocarditis, poor dentition or dental procedure, presence of a prosthetic valve or intracardiac device, history of valvular disease (rheumatic heart disease, mitral valve prolapse, aortic valve disease, mitral regurgitation, etc), congenital heart disease (aortic stenosis, bicuspid aortic valve, pulmonary stenosis, ventricular septal defect, patent ductus arteriosus, coarctation of the aorta, and tetralogy of Fallot), indwelling intravenous catheter, immunosuppression, hemodialysis patients. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Ventricular Septal Defect -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Key facts to keep in mind about VSDs include: This is the most common congenital heart defect in children. Small VSDs often require no intervention, with spontaneous closure being common. The pansystolic murmur of VSD can be mistaken for that of mitral regurgitation; however, the VSD murmur intensifies near the sternum, while the murmur of mitral regurgitation is louder away from the sternum. Prophylactic antibiotics to prevent endocarditis are no longer routinely recommended. Larger or unrepaired VSDs can lead to complications such as arrhythmias, heart failure, and endocarditis. Although antibiotics may not be necessary, good oral hygiene is crucial to reduce the risk of endocarditis.

retrieved_docs.title: Neurological Sequelae of Endocarditis -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Valvular disease such as rheumatic heart disease and congenital heart abnormalities like a bicuspid aortic valve, ventricular septal defect, coarctation of the aorta are risk factors for infectious endocarditis. Pathogens that account for infectious endocarditis vary and depend on the risk factors including intravenous drug abuse, existing valvular dysfunction, poor oral hygiene, and dental procedure, immunocompromised status, the cardiac implantable electronic device (CIED), or previous history of infectious endocarditis. In infectious endocarditis, neurological complications correlate with the size of vegetation and bacterial load. Among all the causative pathogens, Staphylococci aureus is the most common microorganism of infective endocarditis in the United States and other developed countries. Staphylococci aureus IE is significantly more likely to result in embolic events than other pathogens. Neurological sequelae from IE are as following, Embolic stroke is caused by vegetation debris that lodge in the cerebral blood vessels and ended up resulting in infarction; Transient ischemic attack (TIA) is an episode of neurologic dysfunction attributed to ischemia of brain, spinal cord, or retina; Cerebral hemorrhage Meningitis Brain abscess Toxic encephalopathy Seizure

retrieved_docs.title: Endocarditis -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: It is common to find infective endocarditis as a complication of congenital heart disease or rheumatic heart disease. However, is important to mention that infective endocarditis can present in children without any abnormal valves or cardiac malformations.

retrieved_docs.title: Endocarditis -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Infective endocarditis remains high. Morbidity occurs in more than half of children with a diagnosis of infective endocarditis. Most common complications are heart failure secondary to vegetation in the aortic or mitral valve, myocardial abscesses, toxic myocarditis, and life-threatening arrhythmias. Serious complications are systemic emboli, especially affecting the central nervous system. Other complications include a mycotic aneurysm, acquired ventricular septal defect, and heart block. Overall mortality rates range from 7-15%.

retrieved_docs.title: Endocarditis -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: In developed countries, congenital heart disease is the most important predisposing factor. In approximately 30% of patients with infective endocarditis, a predisposing factor is acknowledged and identified.

retrieved_docs.title: Interventricular Septum Abscess -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Echocardiography: Transthoracic echocardiography and transesophageal echocardiography aid in the early diagnosis of the interventricular abscess and infective endocarditis. Transthoracic echocardiography (TTE) is the first echocardiography imaging to use for diagnosis and guide management. TTE has a low sensitivity of 23% and a very high specificity of 98.6% for diagnosing the myocardial abscess. According to a study by Ellis et al., the specific criteria of echocardiography for detection of the myocardial abscess include the following: [27] Evidence of the prosthetic valve rocking Aneurysmal dilatation of the Valsalva sinus Posterior aortic root thickness exceeding 10 mm A higher than 14 mm of perivalvular density in the septum A relatively uncommon but highly specific finding of "echo-free space."

retrieved_docs.title: Aortic Valve Endocarditis -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Infective endocarditis can be community-acquired, or it can be related to healthcare exposure. The epidemiological trends and disease patterns of infective endocarditis have evolved across the globe. In developing countries, rheumatic heart disease remains the key risk factor, affected patients are usually younger, and community-acquired, penicillin-sensitive streptococci typically cause the infection. [1] In the developed world degenerative valve disease, malignancy, intravenous drug use, diabetes mellitus, and congenital heart disease are the predominant risk factors, and the mean age of the patients is older than the ’70s. The cases of healthcare-acquired infective endocarditis due to Staphylococcal infection have surged over the past two decades owing to an increase in the use of invasive procedures, cardiovascular implantable electronic devices, long-term intravenous lines, percutaneous, minimally invasive, and prosthetic heart valves. [2] There has been an increased incidence of infective endocarditis after minimally invasive aortic valve surgery or percutaneous transcatheter aortic valve replacement. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Ventricular Septal Defect -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Routine antibiotic prophylaxis for infective endocarditis in patients with unrepaired VSDs is no longer recommended. [32] Endocarditis prophylaxis is primarily indicated for individuals with cyanotic congenital heart disease, a history of previous endocarditis episodes, those with prosthetic heart valves, or individuals who have undergone repair with prosthetic material.

retrieved_docs.title: Ventricular Septal Defect -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Patients who have undergone VSD repair generally have a favorable prognosis. However, they have an elevated long-term risk of arrhythmia, endocarditis, and heart failure compared to the general population. [36]

retrieved_docs.title: Endocarditis -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Chiefly involves the mitral valve, followed by the aortic valve Congenital heart disease or any defect with high flow tension is susceptible Mitral valve prolapse accounts for about 20% of cases Degenerative heart disease such as the bicuspid aortic valve, Marfan syndrome, or syphilis. Prosthetic valve endocarditis (PVE) Early PVE is caused by S aureus and S epidermidis, including MRSA Streptococci usually cause late PVE Intravenous drug abuse Infective endocarditis: usually presents with a new murmur and/or pleuritic chest pain S.aureus is the most common cause, but MRSA rates are increasing Gram-negative organisms are rarely involved Risk factors Residual valve injury Diabetes Use of steroids Advanced age Pacemaker intervention

retrieved_docs.title: Holt-Oram Syndrome -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Congenital heart malformations are present in 75% of individuals with Holt-Oram syndrome, most commonly ostium secundum atrial septal defect (ASD) and ventricular septal defect (VSD). The septal defects may be mild or can be more severe and lead to further problems such as pulmonary hypertension, congestive heart failure, or infective endocarditis.

retrieved_docs.title: Ventricular Septal Defect -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Complications of VSD may include: Eisenmenger syndrome Aortic insufficiency from aortic valve leaflet prolapse Endocarditis Embolization Heart failure Pulmonary hypertension Arrythmias [37]

retrieved_docs.title: Lutembacher Syndrome -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: In contrast to an isolated ASD, the susceptibility to infective endocarditis is increased by the presence of MS. However, the calcification of mitral valve is less common in LS, as there occurs decompression of LA, resulting in less turbulent flow across the mitral valve. Atrial dilatation in LS predisposes the patients to develop atrial fibrillation.

retrieved_docs.title: ACC/AHA Guidelines for Valve Disease -- Summary / Explanation -- Medical Therapy
retrieved_docs.content: Primary antibiotic prophylaxis is recommended in patients with rheumatic fever to prevent valvular heart disease for over or equal to 10 years or until age 40 (whichever is longer). Antibiotic prophylaxis for infective endocarditis is recommended before dental procedures for patients with prosthetic valves and prosthetic valve repair (rings, chords, or clips), prior infective endocarditis, unrepaired congenital heart disease or repaired congenital heart disease with residual shunts, and cardiac transplant with valvular regurgitation. The duration of antibiotic therapy for secondary prophylaxis of rheumatic fever depends on the presence or absence of valvular heart disease, carditis, and the risk of Group A streptococcus exposure.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1723
retrieved_docs.content: The clinical association between اضطراب النظام (systemic disorder) and تدلي الصمام المترالي (mitral valve prolapse) indicates that patients who exhibit systemic dysregulation may have an increased likelihood of developing mitral valve prolapse, a condition in which the mitral valve leaflets bulge into the left atrium during systole. Recognizing this link helps clinicians consider valvular assessment in individuals with systemic disorders, facilitating earlier diagnosis and more comprehensive management of potential cardiac involvement.

retrieved_docs.content: Mitral valve prolapse (تدلي الصمام المترالي) and congenital vascular dilatation (تمدد الأوعية الدموية الفطري) are both classified as forms of heart valve disease (مرض الصمام القلبي). Recognizing that these conditions fall under the broader category of heart valve disease is important for accurate diagnosis, risk assessment, and the selection of appropriate therapeutic strategies in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1884
retrieved_docs.content: Mitral valve prolapse (تدلي الصمام المترالي) is classified as a form of valvular heart disease (مرض الصمام القلبي), indicating that this condition involves abnormal movement of the mitral valve leaflets and falls within the broader spectrum of diseases affecting cardiac valves. Recognizing mitral valve prolapse as a type of valvular heart disease is essential for accurate diagnosis, risk assessment, and appropriate management of patients with cardiac valve abnormalities.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 2293
retrieved_docs.content: Acute rheumatic carditis, acute rheumatic pancarditis, acute rheumatic valvulitis, الْتِهابُ التَّأَمُوْر الرُّوْمَاتزميّ الحاد (acute rheumatic pericarditis), and الْتِهابُ الشَّغافِ الرُّوْمَاتزميّ الحادّ (acute rheumatic endocarditis) are all manifestations of acute rheumatic heart disease that share a common clinical course characterized by a sudden onset and/or short duration. This abrupt and brief presentation is a key feature across these related conditions, aiding clinicians in recognizing the rapid evolution of rheumatic involvement of the heart’s structures and informing timely diagnostic and therapeutic decisions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2617
retrieved_docs.content: Rheumatic mitral valve obstruction is classified as a structural disorder of the heart, reflecting damage to the mitral valve caused by rheumatic disease that leads to obstruction of blood flow through the valve. Recognizing this condition as a specific type of cardiac structural abnormality is important for accurate diagnosis, appropriate management, and prevention of complications such as heart failure and arrhythmias.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 611
retrieved_docs.content: The heart (srdce) serves as the anatomical target for a spectrum of interventional cardiology procedures—including intracardiac catheter cardioversion, implantation of rate‑responsive single‑ chamber cardiac devices, initial implantation of single‑ chamber devices, removal of a pacemaker with replacement by a single‑ chamber device, removal of pacemaker electrode leads without replacement, and replacement of a pacemaker with a dual‑ chamber device—as well as for diagnostic cardiac catheterization and coronary arteriography. All insertion, revision, replacement, and removal of cardiac pacemakers or cardioverter‑defibrillators are grouped under a single procedural classification for managing implantable cardiac devices. Electrocardiography (تخطيط كهربية القلب ECG) is the primary modality for electrophysiological assessment of the heart during these interventions and for routine cardiac monitoring. The heart is also the parent structure for several specific cardiac pathologies: aortic stenosis (تضيق الأبهر), aortic calcification (تكلس أبهري), and atrial mucous (myxoid) tumors (ورم مخاطي أذيني). Aortic calcification is linked to peripheral and visceral atherosclerosis and constitutes a risk factor for ineffective cerebral tissue perfusion. Similarly, atrial mucinous tumors (atrial myxoma) carry a risk of embolic events that can compromise brain blood flow, leading to ineffective cerebral tissue perfusion. Bacterial myocarditis (التهاب عضل القلب الجرثومي) is categorized within the broader group of peri‑, endo‑, and myocarditis‑related cardiomyopathies. Overall, the integrated view highlights how cardiac interventional and diagnostic techniques, electrophysiological monitoring, device management, and associated vascular and myocardial diseases interrelate in patient care and risk management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2644
retrieved_docs.content: Rheumatic fever (الحُمَّى الروماتيزمية) is clinically associated with the need for aortic valve replacement (استبدال الصمام الأبهري) and coronary valve replacement (استبدال الصمام التاجي). The inflammatory damage caused by rheumatic fever can affect heart valves, leading to severe dysfunction of the aortic and coronary valves that may require surgical replacement. Recognizing this relationship enables clinicians to monitor patients with rheumatic fever for progressive valvular disease and to plan timely surgical intervention, thereby improving patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2099
retrieved_docs.content: Congenital vascular dilatation (تمدد الأوعية الدموية الفطري) is classified as a type of valvular heart disease (مرض الصمام القلبي). This condition involves abnormal dilation of blood vessels that is present from birth and is considered within the spectrum of diseases affecting heart valves. Recognizing congenital vascular dilatation as a valvular heart disease aids clinicians in diagnosing and monitoring potential valve dysfunction, altered hemodynamics, and related complications such as heart failure or arrhythmias, thereby guiding appropriate management and improving patient outcomes.

Inflammatory and systemic cardiac conditions are integrated within the same framework: bacterial myocarditis (التهاب عضل القلب الجرثومي) represents a specific form of peri‑, endo‑, and myocarditis and cardiomyopathy; posterior diabetic network involvement (اعْتِلاَلُ الشَّبَكِيَّةِ السكري الخلفي) and central nervous system vasculitis (التهاب وعائي بالجهاز العصبي المركزي) illustrate the cardiovascular system’s relevance to broader systemic pathologies. The International Classification of Primary Care places primary‑care encounters related to these entities under the cardiovascular system and under “medication, treatment and therapeutic procedures,” underscoring the central role of cardiovascular assessment, monitoring, therapeutic intervention, and

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1782
retrieved_docs.content: Bacterial myocarditis (التهاب عضل القلب الجرثومي) is an overarching infectious cardiac condition that encompasses pericarditis, endocarditis, myocarditis, and cardiomyopathy. These four entities represent inflammation of the pericardium, inflammation of the endocardium, inflammation of the myocardium, and disease of the heart muscle, respectively, and each can arise as a manifestation of bacterial infection of the heart. Understanding that pericarditis, endocarditis, myocarditis, and cardiomyopathy are subcategories of bacterial myocarditis aids clinicians in recognizing the full spectrum of bacterial cardiac involvement and in delivering comprehensive diagnostic and therapeutic care.
